pattern: Since a digit swap is its own reverse, two numbers are within a combined budget of two swaps (spent on either number, in any split) exactly when the set of values reachable from each by zero-or-one swap of its own digits overlaps.
hints: A swap undoes itself: if swapping two positions of A gives B, swapping those same positions of B gives A back. Use that to avoid tracking which number 'spent' which swap.

For every number, precompute the set of values reachable by zero or exactly one swap of its own digit string (letting a swap collapse into a shorter value through a leading zero). Two numbers need at most two combined swaps to match exactly when those two small sets share any value.

Count pairs by scanning left to right: for each number, look up how many earlier numbers have a reachable-set overlap with it (via a value -> earlier-index map), then register this number's own reachable values for future lookups - this avoids the O(n^2) pairwise comparison.

edge_inputs.description: chain of leading-zero relationships across different digit lengths
all duplicates, every pair already equal with zero swaps
a pair that needs one swap on each number (two combined swaps total), not reachable with only one
extreme length gap: 7-digit vs 1-digit needing a swap across the whole string
a 4-digit cyclic permutation that needs more than two combined swaps, so it must NOT count
mixed lengths where only some pairs are truly within the two-swap budget
many small-length duplicate-heavy values to stress tie/duplicate handling

stress.description: random values near max length and max magnitude
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 9999999) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: small-length duplicate-heavy values so many pairs share reachable variants
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    pool = [rng.randint(1, 999) for _ in range(40)]
    nums = [rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

complexity.time: O(n * d^2) average, where d is the number of digits
